title: How long should a block be?
text:
Block maximum method
Idea promoted for engineering applications by Emil Gumbel (cf. Gumbel 1958).

divide a (time) series of length \(b\) into \(n_b\) equal-length disjoint blocks of \(b\) successive observations,
fit the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution to the resulting sample of \(n_b\) block maxima,
use the fitted model for risk assessment.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Motivating example


Figure 1: Daily maximum wind gust speed in Cheeseboro, California, for the months of January (plus Feb. 1st) 1996–2026, for a total of \(32 \times 31\) measurements.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Extremal types theorem
The justification for using the GEV comes from Fisher and Tippett (1928), Gnedenko (1943), von Mises (1954).
If \(G\) is a distribution function and there exist location and scaling sequences \(d_m\) and \(c_m>0\) such that the maximum of \(m\) observations has a non-degenerate limiting distribution \[
\lim_{m \to \infty}G^m(c_mx+d_m) = F(x)
\] then \(F(x)\) must be a GEV distribution function.

title: How long should a block be?
How long should a block be?
How long should a block be?
text:
Stationary setting
The extremal types theorem extends to stationary time series (Leadbetter 1974, 1983; Leadbetter et al. 1983).
Under a condition \(D(u_n)\) and for extremal index \(\theta \in (0,1],\) we get instead \[\begin{align*}
\lim_{m \to \infty} G^{\theta m}(c_{m}x+d_{m})&= F(x)
\end{align*}\] or equivalently \[\begin{align*}
\lim_{m \to \infty} G^{m}(c_{\theta m}x+d_{\theta m}) &= F(x).
\end{align*}\]

title: How long should a block be?
section: Research question: How to choose the block size \(m\)?
text:
Research question: How to choose the block size \(m\)?
Traditional extreme value bias-variance trade-off:

Smaller block sizes may lead to poor GEV approximation (potentially biased extrapolation).
Larger blocks lead to smaller sample sizes (more variability).

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Other considerations

Data availability (frequency of records).
Block maximum is widely viewed as more robust approach in the applied literature.
Theoretical consideration of asymptotic efficiency relative to peaks-over-threshold (Bücher and Zhou 2021).
Natural (minimum) block size (e.g., annual for seasonally-varying data).

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Literature review
Surprisingly, very little work in the area.

Wang et al. (2016) suggest using combinations of goodness-of-fit tests (such as Anderson–Darling or Kolmogorov–Smirnov).
unpublished work of Dkengne et al. (2020) suggests parameter stability plot built using max-stability.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Diagnostic plots


Figure 2: Gumbel plot of \(m\)-day maxima against standard Gumbel plotting positions \(-\log[-\log\{i/(n+1)\}]\) for the Cheeseboro wind gust data.

Plot à la Cox et al. (2002). With Gumbel fit, lines should be parallel, but we see some evidence of distortion for \(b=1\) or \(b=2\) days.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Testing for different block sizes

Consider blocks of size \(b\) versus \(bm\) for \(b, m \in \mathbb{N}.\) We gather observations into an \(n_b/m \times m\) matrix of ordered inputs.





Table 1: Blocks of size \(m=4\) from daily maxima wind gusts (in km/h).

Row \(i\) contains the order statistics \(X_{i, (1)} \leq \cdots \leq X_{i, (m)}.\)

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Joint distribution of order statistics
Let \(X_{(1)} \leq \cdots  \le X_{(m)}\) denote the order statistics.
Let \(F\) and \(f\) be the CDF and density of \(X_j \stackrel{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\mathsf{GEV}(\mu, \sigma, \xi).\)
The joint density of order statistics is the product of

the conditional \(X_{(1)}, \ldots, X_{(m-1)} \mid X_{(m)} = x_{(m)},\)
the marginal \(X_{(m)}\).

title: How long should a block be?
section: Conditional density of lower order statistics

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Distribution of block maximum
The block maximum \(Y=\max(X_1,\ldots, X_m)\) has CDF \(F_{(m)}(x)=F^m(x)\) and density \[\begin{align*}
f_{(m)}(x) &= m F(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi)^{m-1}f(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi) \\
&= f(x; \mu_m, \sigma_m, \xi),
\end{align*}\] by max-stability.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Likelihood

We write \[\begin{align*}
\mathcal{L}({\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_0, {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m)=\prod_{i=1}^n f_{{(1, \ldots, m-1)} \mid {(m)}}(\boldsymbol{x}_{i, -(m)};{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_0)f_{(m)}(x_{i,(m)}; {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m),
\end{align*}\] where

\({\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_0 = (\mu, \sigma, \xi)\) are the parameters for \(X_{i, (1)} \leq \cdots \le X_{i, (m-1)}.\)
\({\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m\) are the parameters of the largest observation \(X_{i, (m)}.\)

Under the null hypothesis of max-stability, \({\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m=(\mu_m, \sigma_m, \xi) = g(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}_0),\) say.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Alternative hypotheses
The idea underlying our testing procedure is to let the parameters of the block maximum differ, by


\(A_1\): setting \(\mathcal{H}_a: {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m = (\mu_{\omega m}, \sigma_{\omega m}, \xi)\) for some scaling \(\omega > 0\), and testing \(\mathcal{H}_0: \omega=1.\)
\(A_2\): allowing both location and scale to vary \(\mathcal{H}_a: {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m = (\mu_m+\nu, \phi \sigma_m, \xi)\) and testing \(\mathcal{H}_0: \nu=0, \phi=1.\)
\(A_3\): letting all three parameters vary \(\mathcal{H}_a: {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m = (\mu_m+\nu, \phi \sigma_m, \xi + \zeta)\) and testing \(\mathcal{H}_0:\nu=0, \phi=1, \zeta=0.\)

We use likelihood ratio statistics to compare the models \(\mathcal{H}_0\) and \(\mathcal{H}_a.\) The asymptotic distribution of the test is \(\chi^2_k\) with \(k\in\{1, 2, 3\}.\)

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Power study stup
We consider different scenarios to assess the power of tests:

sample sizes \(n \in \{25,50,100\},\)
number of replicates \(m\in \{2, 5, 10\},\)
three alternatives \(\mathcal{H}_a.\)

All power curves are based on 2000 replications, with tests conducted at level 5%.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Power study
We consider maxima of

\(\mathsf{normal}(0,1)\) and
\(\mathsf{Weibull}(1, 0.8).\)

with distribution \(G\) and block of size \(30,\) simulate

\(X_{(1)}, \ldots, X_{(m-1)}\) from \(G^{30},\)
\(X_{(m)}\) from \(G^{30\delta},\) but left-truncated at \(x_{(m-1)}\).

title: How long should a block be?
section: Power study - generalized extreme value data
text:
Power study - generalized extreme value data
We use the penultimate approximation to get GEV parameters and simulate from these. Both have limiting shape \(\xi=0,\) but convergence is slow.


Figure 3: GEV parameters based on max-stable (dashed), and the penultimate approximation (full) for Gaussian (top) and Weibull (bottom).

title: How long should a block be?
text: Power curves - GEV

title: How long should a block be?
section: Power study - maximum domain of attraction
text:
Power study - maximum domain of attraction


We simulate directly block maxima from \(G^{30},\) where \(G\) is the Gaussian or Weibull CDF, but where the largest observation is drawn from \(G^{30\delta}.\)

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Summary of power study

As expected, power increases with the sample size \(n\).
Perhaps surprisingly, the test has more power when \(m\) is small!
Alternative 1 is always the more powerful (less degrees of freedom), despite misspecification.
There is a small size distortion (about 5%) for alternative 3 in small samples.

title: How long should a block be?
How long should a block be?
text:
Location parameters of stationary sequences
Max-stability holds asymptotically, but with \(m\theta\) rather than \(m\).


Figure 4: MAR with \(\xi=0\) and \(\theta=0.5\): time series of simulated values (left) and location parameters of the Gumbel maxima based on extrapolating to blocks of size \(m\), taking \(\log(m)\) (iid) and \(\log(m\theta)\) (stationary) as location parameters.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Max-autoregressive process with positive shape

For \(\xi>0\), the first-order max-autoregressive model is \[\begin{align*}
X_i &= \max\{(1-\theta)^\xi X_{i-1}, Z_i\}, \qquad i \geq 1 \\
Z_i &\sim \mathsf{GEV}(\theta^{\xi}, \xi\theta^{\xi}, \xi)
\end{align*}\] The marginal stationary distribution is Fréchet with shape \(\xi^{-1}\), i.e., a \(\mathsf{GEV}(1, \xi, \xi).\)
The distribution of \(Y= \max\{X_1, \ldots, X_{m}\}\) has CDF \(\exp[-\{\theta(m-1)+1\}x^{-1/\xi}];\) see Example 10.3 of Beirlant et al. (2004).

title: How long should a block be?
text: Power curves - max autoregressive

title: How long should a block be?
section: Power study - doubling the block size with MAR
text:
Power study - doubling the block size with MAR

As the empirical block size gets larger, the GEV approximation should become effective and directly incorporate the impact of \(\theta\).

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Comments on power curves

More discrepancies are expected for smaller values of \(\theta,\) as the average cluster size is \(1/\theta,\) the reciprocal of the extremal index.
As the block size increases, the effect of \(\theta\) is captured through parameter estimates and we fail to reject for larger \(\theta,\) as expected.
Alternative 3 suffers again from size distortion, especially in small samples (either when \(m\) or \(n\) is small).

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Diagnostic plots
We propose quantile-quantile plots to assess the goodness-of-fit of the model.
Among quantities of interest

all maxima,
the block maximum \(X_{(m)}\),
the spacing \(X_{(m)} - X_{(m-1)}\).

We use a parametric bootstrap scheme for uncertainty quantification.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Pivots
Under the null hypothesis, the \(F(X_{j}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta})\) are uniform and standard results on order statistics of uniform samples gives for the spacing \[\begin{align*}
S(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}) = F(X_{(m)}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta})- F(X_{(m-1)}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta}) &\sim \mathsf{beta}(1, m)
\end{align*}\] and for the largest observation \[\begin{align*}
F(X_{(m)}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta}) &\sim \mathsf{beta}(m, 1).
\end{align*}\]

Alternative pivots can be obtained, e.g., via Rényi’s representation.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Parametric bootstrap
A conventional parametric bootstrap to test properties of the statistic \(S(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})\) would

simulate uniform draws \(U_{(1)}^* \le \cdots \le U_{(m)}^*.\)
map them to GEV via the quantile function \(X_j^*=F^{-1}(U_i^*; \widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}})\) using the MLE \(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}\), letting \(X^*_{j} \sim \mathsf{GEV}(\widehat{\mu}, \widehat{\sigma}, \widehat{\xi}).\)
obtain maximum likelihood estimates \(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}^{*}\) from \(\boldsymbol{X}^*.\)
use the distribution of the back-transformed values of \(X^{*}_{(m)}\) and \(X^{*}_{(m-1)}\), e.g., \(S(\boldsymbol{X}{}^*; \widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}{}^{*})\)
apply the CDF of the beta distribution to get uniform draws.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Distortion due to plug-in
The null distribution of \(S(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}})\) depends on the unknown true parameter value \(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}\), and although this is replaced by an estimator that is consistent under the null hypothesis, some distortion remains.
The resulting error can be reduced by nested bootstrapping (Davison and Hinkley 1997, sec. 3.9), but this may be too computationally complex for routine use.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Marginal likelihood
Since interest lies in extrapolation, we can also consider estimation of GEV parameters based on the marginal distribution of \(X_{(1)}, \ldots, X_{(m-1)}\), where \[\begin{align*}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{marg}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \prod_{i=1}^n f_{(1),\ldots, (m-1)}(\boldsymbol{x}_i; \boldsymbol{\theta})\\&\propto \prod_{i=1}^n\{1-F(x_{i,(m-1)})\}\prod_{j=1}^{m-1} f(x_{i,(j)};\boldsymbol{\theta})
\end{align*}\] which amounts to right-censoring the largest observation.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
(Dis)advantages of the marginal likelihood

Avoids using the maximum both for model estimation and goodness-of-fit: no double dipping.
Must also include the inequality constraint \(\sigma_0+\xi_0 (\max_{i} x_{i,(m)}-\mu_0) > 0\) on the optimization.



Discarding the largest observation leads to a loss of information: with \(m=4\), we calculate the efficiency for \(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}=(\mu=0, \sigma=1, \xi),\)\[r(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})=\frac{ |\imath_{\text{marg}}(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})|^{1/3}}{|\imath_{\text{full}}(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})|^{1/3}},\] where \(\imath\) is the Fisher information.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Probability-probability plots of maxima


We obtain pointwise and simultaneous confidence intervals using the envelope method (section 4.2.4 of Davison and Hinkley 1997).

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Quantile-quantile plot of all maxima


Work in progress to account for rounding.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Risk extrapolation

The block size can have a sizeable impact on risk estimation (sample maximum is 148km/h). Shape of \(\widehat{\xi}=0.18\) for \(b=1,\) versus \(\xi=-0.09\) for \(b=4.\)


Density of the 50-year maximum for the Cheeseboro wind gust data, based on different block size and maximum likelihood estimates.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Inference for extremes
Use GEV model fitted to blocks of size \(4\) days to calculate risk measures for the maximum 50 years wind gust, by using the max-stability relationship with \(m=8 \times 50.\)

The estimated median of half-century maximum daily wind gust is 139.6 km/h
95% profile-based confidence intervals: (125.9km/h, 168.5km/h).
The serial correlation is negligible.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Summary
We propose likelihood ratio tests for testing max-stability for \(n_b\) blocks of size \(b\)

Build blocks of \(n_b/m \times m\) ordered data.
Check fit to largest (max of \(m\)) and use max-stability to detect departures.
Simulation studies show the test is powerful even in small samples.
The test with a single parameter seems more powerful than competitors, more for \(m=2.\)

Diagnostic plots based on bootstrap to account for goodness-of-fit.

Different values give different indications about the fit (all observations, only the max of blocks of size \(bm\), spacings, etc.) give visual indications of goodness-of-fit.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Limitations

We consider only \(bm\) versus \(b\):

does not allow for comparison of blocks of size 2 vs 3, say.
some data discarded if \(n\) is not divisible by \(bm\) (but could use ideas from Simpson and Northrop (2026)).
in practice it is natural to consider aggregation of periods, e.g., monthly to yearly.

The scheme leads to multiple testing if multiple values of \(m\) and block sizes are considered:

smaller values of \(m\) should lead to higher power, as shown through simulations,
but the choice of \(m\) should be dictated more by practical considerations.

Our approach is restricted to independent or stationary time series.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
Extensions and future work


Accounting for rounding (work in progress) and more guidelines for the choice of graphical diagnostic.
Combining model with estimates of the extremal index \(\theta.\)
Alternative estimators for the GEV built from sliding blocks (e.g., Bücher and Zanger 2023).

Theory for these is more complicated, owing to lack of genuine likelihood.

Could consider Wald-based tests with sandwich-based or bootstrap covariance estimators.

title: How long should a block be?
text:
EVA 2027 - Montréal, July 5th-9th, 2027.

Website: https://hecsciencesdecision.github.io/eva2027/
